text: Hello and welcome to this video documenting a middle lobe microlobectomy.
I think microlobectomy has some great advantages for patients and so I'll talk you through this in this video.
First of all in microlobectomy we're going to restrict ourselves to only 5mm ports in the intercostal spaces.
This first part of the video I'm going to show you how therefore we get into the chest.
It has to be quite different because you can't manually place a finger in the chest cavity.
So I'm going to place a straight camera on a clear port.
It's a CO2 port.
I use CO2 to increase the size of the chest cavity to make things easier.
And this is my setup on entry.

Now this looks non-intuitive but actually it's very straightforward and you can see by the inside view as you push into the chest you can immediately see when you're coming up against the lung the CO2 is then going to push the lung away and you can see by the blunt tip that if there are adhesions you would not see the lung moving away so it's a very safe way to get into the chest.
You then remove the clear introducer and this is a demonstration of exactly how the camera sits as it enters the chest.
This is the view inside for this middle lobectomy.
There are some adhesions around.
Another second important element of microlobectomy is a subxiphoid port.

And it actually is the same calibre as my endodissector, so if I can get my endodissector around a vessel, I'll definitely be able to get the 5mm microcut around it.
And I think this is the second very important element of microlobectomy.
Firstly,
No incisions bigger than five millimeters in the intercostal spaces.
Secondly, we're going to have a sub-xiphoid port and we're going to remove the lobe through a sub-xiphoid incision.
And then thirdly, and very importantly, we're going to do our stapling of vessels with a micro cutter, which makes things so much easier.

So the main reason why I'm wanting to avoid 10 and 12 mil ports in the intercostal spaces is that I don't believe that intercostal spaces are 10 or 12 mils.
I think they are smaller than that, they're 8 mils and I've just been getting a lot of port pain from people and I think it's been because of our large ports
and moving and basically crushing the intercostal nerves with our 10mm ports.
I've been delighted to see absolutely no port pain whatsoever when restricting myself to 5mm ports only in the chest and it's really been having a very significant benefit for patients.
